text: The video shows a surgical view of the lower abdomen, focusing on the umbilical area. The left rectus muscle is visible on the far side, with the cut edge of the posterior rectus sheath partially transected. The surgeon grasps the posterior rectus sheath while dissecting the preperitoneal fat from it. The transverse abdominis muscle is identified, and the surgeon points out the typical TAR plane behind it, aiming to access the fascial plane behind the transverse abdominis for mesh placement under the diaphragm.

The surgeon identifies the scar from a previous left nephrectomy chevron incision and locates the TAR plane beneath the transverse abdominis muscle. Scar tissue is visible from the prior incision. The transverse abdominis muscle is exposed, and the surgeon navigates towards the diaphragm area, noting the transition where the transverse abdominis detaches from the costal margin. The surgeon carefully avoids pulling the fibrous part of the diaphragm down and finds a clear areolar plane deep to the diaphragm muscle, adjacent to the transverse abdominis.

The surgeon points out the transverse abdominis muscle and the fibrous part of the diaphragm, highlighting the plane behind the diaphragm muscle fibers. As the dissection moves medially towards the xiphoid, the surgeon navigates the tricky area, aiming to stay close to the diaphragm muscle fiber. The fibrous layer is visible, and behind it is another layer referred to as the shelf of the diaphragm.

The surgeon performs an upper TAR on the patient's right side, dividing the posterior rectus sheath, which includes the posterior lamella of the internal oblique, transversus abdominis, and diaphragm contribution. The surgeon navigates around the xiphoid, cutting through fat until reaching a natural space, while ensuring to stay behind the fibrous portion of the diaphragm visible on the right side of the screen.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind the fibrous shelf of the diaphragm, ensuring not to pull down diaphragm fibers. The area is sticky, requiring division of some fibers. Muscle fibers are visible, indicating the need to stay deep to avoid anterior diaphragm dissection. The surgeon joins the left and right subdiaphragmatic spaces by cutting the fibrous layer, aiming to access the subdiaphragmatic space without disturbing diaphragm fibers. The left subdiaphragmatic space is dissected, and the surgeon moves behind the xiphoid, staying deep to the shelf, which may vary in presence and position across patients.

The surgeon carefully controls small vessels entering the diaphragm muscle to prevent retraction and bleeding. In the right upper quadrant, the partial TAR is completed by transecting the posterior rectus sheath, including the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and transversus abdominis. The surgeon stays deep to the fibrous layer of the diaphragm, avoiding the costal fibers, while navigating the shelf near the xiphoid.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind the transverse abdominis, cutting through the diaphragm shelf layer on the patient's right side. The peritoneum is sticky and requires traction to separate it from the diaphragm. The surgeon positions the mesh under the diaphragm, ensuring it is well tucked and overlaps the upper edge of the fascial defect to prevent iatrogenic Morgagni hernia.
